perturbation.original_text: immediately prior to offering the Securities to persons in the United States, we had reasonable grounds to believe and did believe that each such person was an Accredited Investor, based upon a pre-existing relationship, and, on the date hereof, we continue to believe that each such offeree purchasing Securities from the Corporation is an Accredited Investor;
perturbation.changed_text: immediately prior to offering the Securities to persons in the United States, we had reasonable grounds to believe and did believe that each such person was an Accredited Investor, regardless of any pre-existing relationship, and, on the date hereof, we continue to believe that each such offeree purchasing Securities from the Corporation is an Accredited Investor;
perturbation.explanation: By removing the pre-existing relationship requirement for accredited investors, the text now contradicts the accredited investor definition under Regulation D.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 506(b)(2)(ii) of Regulation D requires that for offerings under 506(b), there are limitations on who can purchase the securities, specifying the need for a pre-existing relationship unless the investor is an accredited investor. Removing the 'pre-existing relationship' conflicts with this requirement. The change makes it seem as though a relationship is not necessary to classify them as Accredited Investors, whether or not that is the case.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 230.506 - Exemption for limited offers and sales without regard to dollar amount of offering. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 230.506 - Exemption for limited offers and sales without regard to dollar amount of offering. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 230.506 Exemption for limited offers and sales without regard to dollar amount of offering. (a) Exemption. Offers and sales of securities by an issuer that satisfy the conditions in paragraph (b) or (c) of this section shall be deemed to be transactions not involving any public offering within the meaning of section 4(a)(2) of the Act . (b) Conditions to be met in offerings subject to limitation on manner of offering —(1) General conditions. To qualify for an exemption under this section, offers and sales must satisfy all the terms and conditions of §§ 230.501 and 230.502. (2) Specific conditions — (i) Limitation on number of purchasers. There are no more than, or the issuer reasonably believes that there are no more than, 35 purchasers of securities from the issuer in offerings under this section in any 90-calendar-day period. Note 1 to paragraph (b)(2)(i): See § 230.501(e) for the calculation of the number of purchasers and § 230.502(a) for what may or may not constitute an offering under paragraph (b) of this section. (ii) Nature of purchasers. Each purchaser who is not an accredited investor either alone or with his purchaser representative(s) has such knowledge and experience in financial and business matters that he is capable of evaluating the merits and risks of the prospective investment, or the issuer reasonably believes immediately prior to making any sale that such purchaser comes within this description. (c) Conditions to be met in offerings not subject to limitation on manner of offering —(1) General conditions. To qualify for exemption under this section, sales must satisfy all the ter
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the 'pre-existing relationship' requirement, which contradicts the requirement in Rule 506(b)(2)(ii) of Regulation D, as explained in the Law Explanation. This is further supported by Scraped Law Snippet 2, which references 17 CFR § 230.506, covering exemptions for limited offers and sales. While the snippet doesn't explicitly detail the 'pre-existing relationship' requirement, the Law Explanation states it is a requirement under 506(b), making the removal of the relationship a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: no form of Directed Selling Efforts, General Solicitation or General Advertising was used by us in connection with the offer of the Securities by us in the United States, and we have not acted in any manner involving public offering within the meaning of Section 4(a) (2) of the U.S. Securities Act in connection with the offer of the Securities by us in the United States;
perturbation.changed_text: no form of Directed Selling Efforts was intentionally used by us in connection with the offer of the Securities by us in the United States, and we have not knowingly acted in any manner involving public offering within the meaning of Section 4(a) (2) of the U.S. Securities Act in connection with the offer of the Securities by us in the United States;
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'intentionally' and 'knowingly' introduces ambiguity regarding what constitutes Directed Selling Efforts or a public offering. It implies that unintentional actions are permissible, which contradicts SEC regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 4(a)(2) of the Securities Act of 1933
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 4(a)(2) provides an exemption from registration for transactions not involving a public offering. This exemption requires adherence to specific criteria, and the insertion of 'intentionally' and 'knowingly' creates a loophole by suggesting that unintentional directed selling efforts or unknowing involvement in a public offering does not violate the Act, weakening the enforcement of the rule.

Exempted transactions (a) In general The provisions of section 77e of this title shall not apply to- (1) transactions by any person other than an issuer, underwriter, or dealer. (2) transactions by an issuer not involving any public offering. (3) transactions by a dealer (including an underwriter no longer acting as an underwriter in respect of the security involved in such transaction), except- (A
perturbation.justification: The addition of 'intentionally' and 'knowingly' weakens the original obligation. While the snippets provided are general references to the Securities Act of 1933 and Section 4(a)(2) exemptions, the Law Explanation specifies that these additions 'create a loophole by suggesting that unintentional directed selling efforts or unknowing involvement in a public offering does not violate the Act, weakening the enforcement of the rule.' The changed text suggests that liability hinges on intent, undermining the original aim to prevent unregistered securities offerings, regardless of intent.

perturbation.original_text: Terms used in this certificate have the meanings given to them in the Agency Agreement, including Schedule A attached hereto, unless otherwise defined herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Terms used in this certificate have the meanings given to them in the Agency Agreement, including Schedule A attached hereto, regardless of any existing legal definition.
perturbation.explanation: This statement explicitly disregards legally established definitions, prioritizing the contract's definitions, even if they are in conflict with securities law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Act of 1933 and Securities Exchange Act of 1934
perturbation.law_explanation: The Securities Act of 1933 and the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 both establish definitions for terms used in securities transactions. By stating that terms have the meaning defined in the agreement 'regardless of any existing legal definition', the contract attempts to override established law, creating a direct conflict and potential unenforceability.

Definitions; promotion of efficiency, competition, and capital formation (a) Definitions When used in this subchapter, unless the context otherwise requires- (1) The term "security" means any note, stock, treasury stock, security future, security-based swap, bond, debenture, evidence o
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly disregards legally established definitions by stating that terms have the meaning defined in the agreement 'regardless of any existing legal definition'. The Securities Act of 1933 (as indicated by '15 USC 77b: Definitions') and the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 establish definitions for terms used in securities transactions. By attempting to override established law, the contract creates a direct conflict.
